coredata.dc:title: One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Junfen
Zhang, Lin
Li, Pengxia
Zhang, Yan
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon nanodots were synthesized using grape skin as raw material by one step hydrothermal method under optimized experimental conditions. The carbon nanodots were characterized by TEM, fluorescence, UV, IR and XPS. The average particle size of the carbon particles was 4.0±1.5nm. When excited at 430nm, the carbon nanodots showed strong green fluorescence of 522nm which was dependent on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quantum yield reached 18.67%. Stability and anti-photobleaching properties were ideal. Picric acid quenched the fluorescence of the carbon nanodots in the phosphate buffer solution at pH=7.4, and a method of high sensitivity and selectivity to detect picric acid was established. The linear range was 0.06–79.4μM, the correlation coefficient was 0.998, and the detection limit was 10nM (S/N=3). The method was successfully applied to the detection of picric acid in real water samples. The recoveries fell in the range of 95.64%–104.2% and the RSD was 0.26%–0.74%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Grape skin
Picric acid
Fluorescence spectra

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Working curve of picric acid detection
Determination of picric acid in real samples by CDs
Optimization of synthetic conditions of CDs
Working curve of PA with CDs
Selectivity study of CDs and PA detection
Procedure for fluorescence sensing of PA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis process of CDs by grape skin with a simple hydrothermal route and the highly selective and sensitive detection of PA.
(a)TEM image of CDs (the scale is 20nm); (b)TEM image of CDs (the scale is 10nm; inset:the HRTEM image of CDs); (c)Size distribution histogram of CDs(TEM statistics on 150 particles).

UV–vis absorption, excitation (emission at 522nm), and emission (excited at 430nm) spectra of the synthesized carbon nanodots (inset: photographs of CDs solution taken under 365nm UV light and visible light). CCDs =3.8mg/mL.

Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon nanodots at different excitation wavelengths from 360 to 500nm in 10nm increments (right:normalized PL spectra of the CDs). CCDs =52.6μg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a)XPS spectrum of the carbon nanodots. (b) C1S; (c)N1S and (d)O1S spectrum of the carbon nanodots.
Effect of the pH(a) and time(b) to reaction of carbon nanodots and PA. CCDs =50μg/mL,CPA =9.9μM.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon nanodots in the presence of different concentrations of PA (Inset a: the relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and the PA concentration. Inset b: the linear plot of 1-F/F0 and the concentration of PA within the linear range of 0–79.4μM).CCDs =50μg/mL.

Fluorescence intensity of carbon nanodots in the presence of different (a) nitroaromatics and phenols; (b) metal ions in PBS buffer(pH 7.4) and PA. Concentrations of PA, nitroaromatics, phenols and the metal ions are all 10μM.CCDs =52.6μg/mL.
Detailed information of nitro and phenolic compounds involved in the experiments.
Comparison of different methods for PA detection.
Determination of PA in real water samples(n=3).

head.ce:title: One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Environmental-science and Engineering-study Center, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon nanodots were synthesized using grape skin as raw material by one step hydrothermal method under optimized experimental conditions. The carbon nanodots were characterized by TEM, fluorescence, UV, IR and XPS. The average particle size of the carbon particles was 4.0±1.5nm. When excited at 430nm, the carbon nanodots showed strong green fluorescence of 522nm which was dependent on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quantum yield reached 18.67%. Stability and anti-photobleaching properties were ideal. Picric acid quenched the fluorescence of the carbon nanodots in the phosphate buffer solution at pH=7.4, and a method of high sensitivity and selectivity to detect picric acid was established. The linear range was 0.06–79.4μM, the correlation coefficient was 0.998, and the detection limit was 10nM (S/N=3). The method was successfully applied to the detection of picric acid in real water samples. The recoveries fell in the range of 95.64%–104.2% and the RSD was 0.26%–0.74%.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nano particles less than 10nm in size [1], and are dispersed and quasi-spherical. CDs were first observed by Xu et al., who observed single walled carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis in 2004 [2]. On account of many excellent features, such as small size, tunable emission wavelength [3], strong photostability [4], biocompatibility [5,6], two-photon absorption, optical charge transfer, near infrared photoluminescence and electroluminescence characteristics [7]. CDs are widely used in the field of carbon catalytics, optical devices, fluorescent sensors and bioimaging [8–10]. People are now exploring ways to synthesize CDs, via top-down and bottom-up methods according to different carbon sources [11]. The top-down approach is mainly performed by chemical oxidation or by nodular or mechanical crushing of activated carbon to obtain nano scale precursor carbon–such as nano diamond, carbon black, graphite, carbon nanotubes, ash, and other large candle carbon material. The main methods involved include discharge [12], laser ablation [13], electrochemical [14,15] and the direct synthesis of carbonation [16]. The “bottom-up” method mainly refers to the production of nano particles under different experimental conditions by using small molecules as precursors. These experimental conditions include pyrolysis [17], microwave assisted [18], ultrasonic assisted [19], hydrothermal mechanisms [20,21] and alternatives. The hydrothermal method has been the most widely used because of its simplicity and is also environmentally friendly [22].

Grape skin contains large amounts of anthocyanin, resveratrol, cellulose pectic substances and so on [23]. Anthocyanin is particularly good in preventing various diseases associated with free radicals. Resveratrol can prevent cardiac weakness, cerebrovascular disease, and skin cancer [24]. Therefore, wine, grape juice and other drinks are becoming more and more popular. In this paper, the grape skin was used as a new carbon source to synthesize CDs via an economic and facial synthesis method.

Picric acid (abbreviated PA or TNP, C6H3N3O7), has been widely used in the manufacture of pesticides, chemical fibers, explosives, fireworks, and etc. It could lead to water and soil pollution if discharged into the environment [25]. PA can cause headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting and other symptoms in humans when breathed in. In addition, it has the hazards of causing contact dermatitis, conjunctivitis, and bronchitis [26]. China's “drinking water health standards” (GB5749-2006) [27] and “surface water environmental quality standards” (GB3838-2002) [28] regulate that the maximum allowable concentration of PA in water is 0.5mg/L. In general, methods used to detect PA are mass spectrometry [29] surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [30,31], field effect transistor (FET) [32] and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) [33,34]. Because of the structure similarity, it is difficult to distinguish PA from other nitro compounds by photoinduced electron transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, strong electrostatic interaction and internal filtering effect mechanism [35]. These methods are thus limited by poor selectivity or complicated procedures. The sensitivity of these tests still need to be improved so it is of great significance to establish a simple, highly sensitive and fast responding method to test PA in water.

In this paper, we used grape skin as a carbon source and synthesized carbon nanodots using the hydrothermal synthesis method, and had a fluorescence quantum yield of 18.67%. The method of detecting PA was established based on fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots synthesized through picric acid in phosphate buffer solution at pH=7.4. The procedure is shown in Fig. 1 . Additionally, the method was applied to real water samples. Low detection limit, high sensitivity, great selectivity and good anti-jamming capability were obtained.

Grapes (bought from supermarket), rhodamine 6G was purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company, picric acid (PA, 0.01M) was purchased from Dalian Dyestuff Factory, PBS buffer solution (concentration was 10 mmoL, pH 1–12), nitro compounds, phenolic compounds and metal ions solutions (concentration was 0.1M). All these reagents were domestic and pure and were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company; experimental water was double distilled water.

The UV absorption spectra of carbon nanodots were obtained on an UV-265 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with a 1cm quart cell. IR spectrum of CDs was recorded with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Corporation, USA) with solid powder that received by freeze-drying (Ningbo Xinzhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). The fluorescence spectra of CDs was collected on a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) by a 1cm fluorescent colorimetric cell. The excitation/emission slits were set to 10nm/10nm, and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 430nm and 522nm, respectively. The JEM-2100 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (Japan JEOL) scanning electron microscope was used to acquire the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images at an accelerating voltage of 20KV. The KQ5200B ultrasonic cleaning (Kunshan City ultrasound Instrument Co., Ltd.) was taken with gain solution and weighs solid with Analytical Balance (Austrian House Instrument Co., Ltd.). A PHS-3C pH meter (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) was utilized to measu Microscope (Japan JEOL) scanning electron microscope was used to acquire the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images at an accelerating voltage of 20KV. The KQ5200B ultrasonic cleaning (Kunshan City ultrasound Instrument Co., Ltd.) was taken with gain solution and weighs solid with Analytical Balance (Austrian House Instrument Co., Ltd.). A PHS-3C pH meter (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) was utilized to measure the pH values. The elemental composition of CDs was examined by an Axis Ultra Dld X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD, Britain).

The CDs were synthesized from grape skin through a simple, convenient one-step hydrothermal method. The grape skin was cleaned by double distilled water and dried at 40°C. Then 1.0g of grape skin and 30mL of double distilled water were kept in a 50mL Teflon equipped stainless-steel autoclave and was heated at a constant temperature of 190°C for 3h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered and the supernatant fraction was centrifuged at 13000rpm for 10min. Dialysis was undertaken with a MW1000 dialysis bag for 24h and freeze-dried. Then, the solid brown fluorescent CDs were obtained for further characterization and use [20].

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of a CD solution with Rhodamine 6G[21] (excitation wavelength: 488nm, quantum yield 0.94, dissolved in ethanol). In an effort to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of the CDs solution was kept below 0.05. The QY of CDs was calculated from the following equation:
Yu=Ys* Fu/Fs* As/Au* (ηu/ηs)2
here, Yu and Ys show the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs solution and the standard substance at the excitation wavelength. Fu and Fs are the integrated fluorescence intensities of the CDs solution and the standard substance. Additionally ηu and ηs stand for the refractive index of the CDs solution to be tested and the reference standard substance (the refractive index of water was 1.3329 and alcohol was 1.3614).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working curve of picric acid detection
ce:section.ce:para: 0.0114g of picric acid was dissolved in 5mL hot water to form a 0.01M solution. 40μL of CDs solution was diluted with 1mL of PBS buffer (pH=7.4) and various amounts of picric acid solution was added to form different concentrations. Their fluorescence intensities were carried out at an excitation wavelength of 430nm. Meanwhile, the spectrum of reagent blank was recorded under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of picric acid in real samples by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water, fish pond water and LingDe Lake water were all collected from ShanXi University and were used as the actual samples. First, the real samples were filtered, then buffered in 0.5mL of PBS buffer solution (pH=7.4), then this was mixed with 40μL of carbon solution evenly to detect the concentration of picric acid in the samples. Subsequently, standard solutions of PA of different concentrations was added in the system to detect the fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 430nm, and the standard recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthetic conditions of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction temperature, reaction time and the amount of grape skin were investigated to obtain optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. S1a, the fluorescence intensity of carbon nanodots increased with an increase in reaction temperature. When the temperature was too high, the surface structure of the CDs would be destroyed and the fluorescence emission was reduced. However, the carbon particle size was hardly affected because of the stability of emission peak [20]. The optimum reaction temperature was 190°C, reaction time was selected as 3h (as shown in Fig. S1b), and the optimum reaction amount of grape skin was 1.0g (Fig. S1c).

TEM image was applied to reveal the morphologies of CDs (Fig. 2 ). It can be clearly seen that the synthesized CDs are quasi spherical with good monodispersity. Fig. b illustrates the HRTEM graph, where lattice spacing is about 0.203nm, which indicates a high degree of crystallization between the CDs near the graphite (002) surface [36]. Fig. c shows the particle size distribution of the CDs (by counting 150 particles), the average carbon particle size was 4.0±1.5nm.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy and there were two strong absorption peaks at 210nm and 280nm, respectively (Fig. 3 ), in UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peak at 252nm shows simultaneous π–π* transition of the CC structure and n-π* transition to CO on the CDs shells. Just as the fluorescence spectra indicates (Fig. 3), the maximum excitation wavelength of the synthesized CDs is 430nm, and the corresponding optimum emission wavelength is 522nm to give off green fluorescence.

Fluorescence emission spectrum was measured every 10nm in range of the excitation wavelengths from 360nm to 500nm and is displayed in Fig. 4 . From this figure, we can see that the fluorescence intensity increases gradually while the excitation wavelength increases from 360nm to 430nm, and then decreases when the excitation wavelength exceeds 430nm – thus the peak excitation wavelength is 430nm. Meanwhile, as the excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelength red shifts from 452nm to 532nm. This satisfies the characteristics of carbon, particularly its excitation wavelength dependence. In other words, it has multiple excitations and emissions, which may be caused by different emission sites off the carbon surface.

The infrared spectra of the carbon dots are shown in Fig. S2. The peaks in the range of 3011–3611cm−1 can be attributed to the characteristic absorption bands of the stretching vibration of OH and NH, respectively. The peaks at 2927.70 and 1304.72cm−1 can be described as the stretching vibration and bending vibration of saturated CH; the peak at about 1671.01cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the CO; the wide absorption peak of OH at 3308.27cm−1 indicates the OH bending vibration on COOH; the peak at 1588.12cm−1 implies the existence of CC groups; the two absorption peaks at 1261.57 and 1064.24cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of COH, respectively; and the peak at 1210.59cm−1 indicates the presence of COC stretching vibration [36,37]. These peaks indicate that carbon nanodots have favorable properties of stability and dispersibility in water. This result reveals that the synthesized carbon nanodots contain CC, amine and carboxylic groups.

XPS analysis was used to investigate the surface element construction of CDs. Fig. 5 a displays three typical peaks at 283.0eV, 398.0eV and 530.0eV, which could be attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. This indicates the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The proportions of C was 44.04%, N was 29.71% and O was 26.25% (C: O: N atomic ratio were 2.04: 1.02: 1.04, respectively). High resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b) consists of four main peaks centered at 284.6eV, 285.2eV, 286.2eV and 288.2eV, which corresponds to CC, CN, CC, and OCO, respectively. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5c) can be resolved into two distinct carbon states at 400.1eV and 401.0eV and can be linked to the CN and NH. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) reveals the signals of CO at 532.4eV and OCO at 533.4eV [38]. This result indicates that the synthesized carbon nanodots contain amine and carboxylic groups.

CDs stability is a particularly significant characteristic and could prevent the CDs from interfering with other external conditions. The effects of various concentrations of NaCl solution, H2O2, pH and storage time [39] on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated (Fig. S3). As shown in figure a and figure b, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was almost invariable in comparatively high ionic strength and hydrogen peroxide concentration–the CDs had great salt resistance and antioxidant properties. Meanwhile, it can be seen from Fig. S3c that the fluorescence intensity increased with an increase in pH, and then remained stable from pH 7.4–11.0. Thus, 7.4 was selected as the optimum pH. This pH-dependent fluorescence emission is a result of the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl or amino groups on the surface of the CDs. This in turn caused electrostatic doping/charging of the CDs and shifting to the Fermi level. Fig. S3d showed that the CDs were stable over 2 months at 4°C. Above all, these results indicate that the CDs are conducive to the detection of picric acid in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working curve of PA with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect PA accurately, it is necessary to optimize the assay conditions, specifically pH value and reaction time between CDs and PA. Both conditions were studied by detecting their fluorescence quenching efficiency: 1-F/F0, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of PA, respectively. PA was measured to be non-fluorescent. The effects of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the system were investigated just as shown in Fig. 6 a. The corresponding fluorescence quench is most obvious at pH 7.4, and thus, this buffer solution was selected as the most optimal pH value in the PA sensing procedure for follow-up studies. Under the same conditions, the fluorescence intensity was measured every 20s, and the recorded data has been shown in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity was stable after 20s, indicating that the response time was rapid.

To explore the PA concentration dependent fluorescence intensity property of CDs, various concentrations of PA were added with the proposed method under optimum conditions. Fig. 7 a displays that fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with an increase in PA concentration from 0 to 566μM, then would stabilize. The carboxyl groups on the carbon nanodots and the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the picric acid can undergo dehydration condensation. In addition, electron transferred from the donor, amino group on CDs to the acceptor, nitro group on PA. These processes led to fluorescence quench, which accelerated the non-radiative electron/hole recombination of excitation. This mechanism was in agreement with what Fan et al. [39] outlined.

There was a good linear relationship between analytical signal of 1−F/F0 and the concentration of PA from 0.06 to 79.4μM (Fig. 7b). The linear correlation can be shown by the linear regression equation 1−F/F0 =0.01079C(PA) +0.0206. Its detection limit (3s/κ) was 10nM at a signal to noise ratio of 3, and the correlation coefficient R2 was 0.998.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of CDs and PA detection
Procedure for fluorescence sensing of PA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to figure out the feasibility of the proposed method, this method was used to detect the content of PA in three kinds of real samples from Shanxi University, tap water of the chemical laboratory, pond water in the Science Building and LingDe lake, and water samples 1, 2 and 3. The measurement results were 0.3560mg/L, 0.3949mg/L, 0.4955mg/L, respectively. As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries range from 95.64% to 104.2% after adding a given amount of PA in standard solutions, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of three repeated detections for each sample are below 0.74%. The detection was not influenced by the constituents in the detection medium, especially in real samples. This indicates that CDs are a promising means of detecting PA in real samples.

In this work, CDs were synthesized successfully through a simple, economic and environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermal method. The CDs had excellent solubility, oxidation resistance, stability and high quantum yield (18.67%). It was found that the initial fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by PA due to a charge transfer process. Thus, these CDs were an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of PA in this paper. The method was successfully applied to real water samples and tap water samples from the chemical laboratory and was discovered to have advantageous characteristics including high sensitivity, high selectivity, and a low detection limit of 10nM. We hope that the method could be applied to assay PA in other samples except for water samples in the further study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 20575084) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201701D121021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.096.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

The production of pH-sensitive photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles by the carbonization of polyethylenimine and their use for bioimaging

White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots electroluminescence
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Plant leaf-derived graphene quantum dots and applications for white LEDs
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon nanoparticles as chromophores for photon harvesting and photoconversion
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes(MWCNTs)
Eleclrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
Naked oats derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging
One—step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots in noncoordinating solvents
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli
Blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots from limeade
Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications
Preparative separation of grape skin polyphenols by high-speed counter-current chromatography
Linking ATR-FTIR and Raman features to phenolic extractability and other attributes in grape skin
Selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on a fluorescent nanoscale bis(8-hydroxyquinoline) metal complex
BODIPY based fluorescent chemodosimeter for explosive picric acid in aqueous media and rapid detection in the solid state

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: GB 5749-2006. Sanitary Standard for Drinking Water National Standards of the People's Republic of China.
GB 3 838-2002. Surface Water Environmental Quality Standards. National Standards of the People's Republic of China.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion chromatography-mass spectrometry: a review of recent technologies and applications in forensic and environmental explosives analysis
Imprinted Au-nanoparticle composites for the ultrasensitive surface plasmon resonance detection of hexahydro-1,3,5-trinitro-1,3,5-triazine (RDX)
Ultrasensitive surface plasmon resonance detection of trinitrotoluene by a bis-aniline-cross-linked Au nanoparticles composite
Highly ordered binary assembly of silica mesochannels and surfactant micelles for extraction and electrochemical analysis of trace nitroaromatic explosives and pesticides
Highly selective detection of trinitrophenol by luminescent functionalized reduced graphene oxide through FRET mechanism
Highly selective and sensitive fluorescent paper sensor for nitroaromatic explosive detection
Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue-green photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Rapid microwave synthesis of N-doped carbon nanodots with high fluorescence brightness for cell imaging and sensitive detection of iron(III)
Sensitive detection of picric acid based on creatinine-capped solid film assembled by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and chitosan
N, B-doped carbon dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for Hg2+ ions and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection for bioimaging
A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid
Highly sensitive fluorescent imidazolium-based sensors for nanomolar detection of explosive picric acid in aqueous medium
Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon nanoparticles as photoluminescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of picric acid

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junfen Li is an associate professor in the Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry in 2008 at Shanxi University (China). Her research interests are photochemical sensors and biosensors.
Lin Zhang is currently doing her M.S. at the School of Chemistry, in the Shanxi University. Her research interest is the synthesis of carbon nanodots and the sensor applications.
Pengxia Li is currently doing her M.S. at the School of Chemistry, in the Shanxi University. Her research interest is the synthesis of carbon nanodots and the sensor applications.

Yan Zhang earned her BSc in chemistry and Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry in 2004 and 2009 from Shanxi University, respectively. She is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University and her current research interests include the nanomaterial and biosensors analysis.

Chuan Dong is a professor of analytical chemistry in the Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University. He received his Ph.D. degree in 2002 from Shanxi University. His current research interests include biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensors, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.
